title: http (package)
objectives: Make simple GET/POST calls with the http package
Know when to graduate to Dio/Retrofit

sections.markdown: `package:http` is Dart's official, minimal HTTP client — just enough for straightforward REST calls. Small, no ceremony, works everywhere Dart runs (mobile, desktop, web, server).
Reuse a single `http.Client` for many requests — it pools connections (HTTP keep-alive). Creating a new client per call defeats connection reuse and can hurt latency, especially over TLS.
Manually building interceptor logic in every call site (auth headers, retries). At that point graduate to Dio or wrap `http.Client` in a middleware pattern (`BaseClient`).
For simple apps or packages that want minimal dependencies, `http` is perfect. For complex flows (auth refresh, retries, cancellation, progress), Dio saves a lot of code.

flashcards.front: http vs dio in one line?
flashcards.back: http is small and official — great for simple calls. Dio has interceptors/cancellation/progress and scales to complex apps.

flashcards.front: Should you create a new Client per request?
flashcards.back: No — reuse one Client to pool TCP/TLS connections.

flashcards.front: How to add global behavior with http?
flashcards.back: Subclass BaseClient and wrap sends (middleware pattern).

quiz.explain: Connection reuse cuts TLS + TCP overhead.

quiz.q: For interceptors/retries at scale you'd typically…
quiz.explain: Dio provides them out of the box.

challenges.text: GET a URL and pretty-print the JSON.
POST a JSON body with headers; handle errors.
Wrap http.Client in a BaseClient adding an Authorization header.
Add retry-with-backoff via a BaseClient wrapper.

interviewQuestions: http vs dio — when use each?
How would you add auth to every call?
How do you test code using http.Client?
How to handle timeouts with http?
